does: This paper develops RA-PbRL, an algorithm for preference-based reinforcement learning that optimizes risk-aware objectives rather than just mean reward. The work proves the applicability of nested and static quantile risk objectives to the preference-learning setting, which is particularly relevant for scenarios where a single episode's reward matters (one-episode-reward setting). The authors provide theoretical regret upper bounds for their algorithm, establishing that risk-aware optimization is computationally tractable in the preference-based learning framework. The approach directly addresses the gap between the widespread use of human feedback in RLHF and the lack of formal guarantees about safe, risk-sensitive behavior.
connects: This work bridges preference-based reinforcement learning and risk-sensitive RL, two traditionally separate research areas. It extends prior work on RLHF and PbRL—which conventionally optimize expected reward—by incorporating quantile-based risk measures that have been studied in the risk-sensitive RL literature. The connection to CVaR and quantile objectives draws on established safety-motivated RL work, while the preference-learning formulation builds on recent human-feedback approaches popularized by large language model alignment. The paper responds to the recognized limitation that standard RLHF methods lack explicit safety guarantees, particularly in one-episode settings common in critical applications like healthcare and autonomous systems.
enables: This framework enables practical deployment of preference-based learning systems in safety-critical domains where worst-case or tail-risk performance matters. The theoretical regret bounds open avenues for developing more sophisticated risk-aware learning algorithms with provable guarantees. Future work can extend these results to multi-episode settings, explore combinations with other safety constraints, and apply the framework to real-world RLHF systems for language models and robotic control. The approach also suggests connections between formal safety analysis and human preference learning, potentially leading to better alignment methods that explicitly account for distributional safety requirements rather than optimizing only average performance.
